abstract:
Policy advisory systems, designed to provide evidence-informed guidance to governments, face immense pressure during times of sudden change and turbulence. Crises often trigger conflicting interpretations among stakeholders regarding the best course of action, disputes that are further aggravated by inevitable gaps in data and uncertainty about the future. While modern governments face a diverse array of disruptions—ranging from financial collapses and national security threats to natural disasters—the COVID-19 pandemic placed these decision-making pressures under an unprecedented global spotlight.

This introductory article frames a collection of research studies that offer fresh insights into how governmental advisory systems navigated the pandemic and other emergencies. Policy advice was frequently developed amidst heated controversies over how to balance public health priorities against economic stability and social liberty. The included studies evaluate these systems by examining the quality of available knowledge, the disciplinary diversity of expert bodies, and the evolving roles of experts—whether acting as trusted internal advisors, external critics, or strategic "shapers" of the policy narrative. Furthermore, the articles explore the tension between formal administrative processes and informal political relationships. 

By extending the analysis to "creeping crises" like climate change and antimicrobial resistance, and including perspectives from both OECD and low-income countries, this special issue advances our understanding of how expert knowledge informs crisis governance. Ultimately, it clarifies the complex, often shifting boundaries between scientific expertise and political decision-making in an increasingly unstable world.
